\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\begin{document}

\section{Continuity and Uniform Continuity}

\begin{definition}\label{def:continuous}
A function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is \emph{continuous} at a point
$a \in \mathbb{R}$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$
such that $|f(x) - f(a)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x - a| < \delta$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:uniform-continuous}
A function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is \emph{uniformly continuous}
if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
$|f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x - y| < \delta$,
for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:uniform-implies-continuous}
If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R}$, then $f$ is continuous at
every point $a \in \mathbb{R}$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $\varepsilon > 0$. Since $f$ is uniformly continuous, there exists
$\delta > 0$ such that $|f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x - y| < \delta$.
In particular, fixing $y = a$, we get $|f(x) - f(a)| < \varepsilon$
whenever $|x - a| < \delta$.
\end{proof}

\section{Compactness}

\begin{definition}\label{def:compact}
A subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is \emph{compact} if every open cover
of $K$ has a finite subcover.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:heine-borel}
A subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if it is
closed and bounded.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We prove both directions separately.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{($\Rightarrow$) Compact implies closed and bounded.}

\smallskip
\textit{Boundedness.}
Consider the open cover $\{ B(\mathbf{0}, m) \}_{m=1}^{\infty}$ of
$\mathbb{R}^n$, where $B(\mathbf{0}, m)$ denotes the open ball of radius
$m$ centred at the origin.  Restricting to $K$ gives an open cover of $K$.
By compactness there is a finite subcover
$B(\mathbf{0}, m_1), \dots, B(\mathbf{0}, m_k)$.
Setting $M = \max\{m_1, \dots, m_k\}$ we obtain $K \subseteq B(\mathbf{0}, M)$,
so $K$ is bounded.

\smallskip
\textit{Closedness.}
We show that $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus K$ is open.
Let $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \setminus K$ be arbitrary.
For each $\mathbf{y} \in K$, let $r_{\mathbf{y}} = \tfrac{1}{2}
\|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\| > 0$ and define open balls
\[
    U_{\mathbf{y}} = B\!\left(\mathbf{y},\, r_{\mathbf{y}}\right),
    \qquad
    V_{\mathbf{y}} = B\!\left(\mathbf{x},\, r_{\mathbf{y}}\right).
\]
The triangle inequality ensures $U_{\mathbf{y}} \cap V_{\mathbf{y}} = \emptyset$
for every $\mathbf{y}$.
The family $\{U_{\mathbf{y}}\}_{\mathbf{y} \in K}$ is an open cover of $K$,
so by compactness it has a finite subcover
$U_{\mathbf{y}_1}, \dots, U_{\mathbf{y}_m}$.
Set
\[
    V = \bigcap_{i=1}^{m} V_{\mathbf{y}_i},
\]
which is a finite intersection of open balls containing $\mathbf{x}$,
hence itself an open neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}$.
For any $\mathbf{z} \in V$ and any $i$, we have
$\mathbf{z} \in V_{\mathbf{y}_i}$ and $V_{\mathbf{y}_i} \cap U_{\mathbf{y}_i} = \emptyset$,
so $\mathbf{z} \notin U_{\mathbf{y}_i}$.
But every point of $K$ lies in some $U_{\mathbf{y}_i}$, so $\mathbf{z} \notin K$.
Thus $V \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \setminus K$, proving $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus K$
is open.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{($\Leftarrow$) Closed and bounded implies compact.}

\smallskip
\textit{Step 1: Every closed bounded interval $[a, b] \subset \mathbb{R}$ is compact.}

Suppose for contradiction that $\mathcal{U} = \{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in A}$ is an
open cover of $[a, b]$ that admits no finite subcover.
Set $I_0 = [a, b]$ and $c_0 = (a+b)/2$.
At least one of $[a, c_0]$ or $[c_0, b]$ cannot be covered by finitely many
members of $\mathcal{U}$; call it $I_1 = [a_1, b_1]$.
Repeat: bisect $I_1$ and choose the half that has no finite subcover, calling
it $I_2 = [a_2, b_2]$.
Continuing inductively we obtain a nested sequence of closed intervals
\[
    I_0 \supseteq I_1 \supseteq I_2 \supseteq \cdots,
    \qquad |I_k| = \frac{b-a}{2^k},
\]
each of which has no finite subcover from $\mathcal{U}$.
The sequence of left endpoints $(a_k)$ is increasing and bounded above by
$b$, so it converges to some $c \in [a, b]$ by the completeness of
$\mathbb{R}$.
Similarly the right endpoints satisfy $b_k \to c$.
Since $c \in [a, b]$, there exists some $U_{\alpha_0} \in \mathcal{U}$
with $c \in U_{\alpha_0}$.
As $U_{\alpha_0}$ is open, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that
$(c - \varepsilon, c + \varepsilon) \subseteq U_{\alpha_0}$.
Choose $k$ large enough that $|I_k| = (b-a)/2^k < \varepsilon/2$ and
$|c - a_k| < \varepsilon/2$.
Then $I_k \subseteq (c - \varepsilon, c + \varepsilon) \subseteq U_{\alpha_0}$,
contradicting the construction that $I_k$ has no finite subcover.
Hence $[a, b]$ is compact.

\smallskip
\textit{Step 2: Every closed box $[-M, M]^n \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is compact.}

Let $\mathcal{U}$ be an open cover of $Q_0 = [-M, M]^n$.
If $Q_0$ has no finite subcover, subdivide it into $2^n$ equal sub-boxes
by bisecting each coordinate interval.
At least one sub-box $Q_1$ has no finite subcover; otherwise finitely many
boxes (each with a finite subcover) would cover $Q_0$.
Continue inductively: at stage $k$ subdivide $Q_{k-1}$ into $2^n$ equal
sub-boxes and select $Q_k$ admitting no finite subcover.
The diameters satisfy $\operatorname{diam}(Q_k) = \sqrt{n}\,M/2^{k-1} \to 0$.
By the completeness of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and the finite intersection property
for closed sets, $\bigcap_{k=0}^\infty Q_k = \{\mathbf{c}\}$ for some
$\mathbf{c} \in Q_0$.
Choose $U_{\alpha_0} \in \mathcal{U}$ with $\mathbf{c} \in U_{\alpha_0}$ and
$\varepsilon > 0$ with $B(\mathbf{c}, \varepsilon) \subseteq U_{\alpha_0}$.
For all sufficiently large $k$ we have
$Q_k \subseteq B(\mathbf{c}, \varepsilon) \subseteq U_{\alpha_0}$,
contradicting the choice of $Q_k$.
Hence $Q_0$ is compact.

\smallskip
\textit{Step 3: Conclusion.}

Since $K$ is bounded, there exists $M > 0$ such that
$K \subseteq [-M, M]^n$.
The box $[-M, M]^n$ is compact by Step~2.
A closed subset of a compact set is compact: if $\mathcal{U}$ is an open
cover of $K$, then $\mathcal{U} \cup \{\mathbb{R}^n \setminus K\}$ is an open
cover of $[-M, M]^n$; a finite subcover of $[-M, M]^n$ restricted to $K$
(discarding $\mathbb{R}^n \setminus K$) yields a finite subcover of $K$.
Hence $K$ is compact.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:compact-continuous}
A continuous function on a compact set attains its maximum and minimum.
\end{corollary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:uniform-on-compact}
A continuous function $f$ on a compact set $K$ is uniformly continuous.
\end{lemma}

\section{Integration}

\begin{definition}\label{def:riemann-integral}
The \emph{Riemann integral} of $f$ over $[a, b]$ is defined as
\[
    \int_a^b f(x)\, dx = \lim_{\|\mathcal{P}\| \to 0} \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(\xi_i)\,\Delta x_i
\]
where $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $[a, b]$ and $\xi_i$ are sample points.
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:ftc}
Let $f$ be continuous on $[a, b]$ and let $F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\, dt$.
Then $F$ is differentiable and $F'(x) = f(x)$ for all $x \in [a, b]$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{remark}
This is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. It connects differentiation
and integration as inverse operations.
\end{remark}
